main_focus: Viewer TLS security policy; viewer/origin HTTPS; ACM/IAM certificate binding
typical_risks: Legacy viewer security policies such as SSLv3, TLS 1.0, or TLS 1.1; viewer or origin HTTP paths; weak origin TLS versions; expired, mismatched, or incomplete certificates.
service_intro: CloudFront cryptography configuration focuses on TLS/HTTPS from viewers to edge locations, from edge locations to origins, and on custom-domain certificate governance.

service: Elastic Load Balancing - Application Load Balancer (ALB)
main_focus: HTTPS listener SslPolicy; listener certificate; SNI certificate list; mutual TLS
typical_risks: Legacy TLS policies, weak compatibility ciphers, FIPS legacy policy misuse, implicit default policies during automation, and certificate or client-certificate trust issues.
service_intro: ALB cryptography configuration centers on HTTPS listener SslPolicy, certificates, SNI certificate lists, and optional mutual TLS.

service: Elastic Load Balancing - Network Load Balancer (NLB)
main_focus: TLS listener SslPolicy; listener certificate; ALPN policy; TLS offload versus TCP pass-through
typical_risks: Legacy TLS policies, weak compatibility ciphers, unmanaged certificates, protocol-mode confusion, and mismatch between TLS offload and end-to-end encryption requirements.
service_intro: NLB cryptography configuration focuses on TLS listener SslPolicy, listener certificates, ALPN, and the choice between TLS offload and TCP pass-through.

main_focus: VPC Encryption Controls; Flow Logs encryption-status; Transit Gateway encryption support; encryption exclusions
typical_risks: VPC Encryption Controls disabled, insufficient encryption-status visibility, long-lived monitored cleartext flows, enforcement before remediation, and unmanaged exclusions.
service_intro: Amazon VPC is not a TLS policy or cipher-suite configuration surface, but it can support in-transit encryption governance through VPC Encryption Controls, Flow Logs, and Transit Gateway encryption support.

main_focus: DNSSEC signing; key-signing key (KSK); AWS KMS asymmetric key; DS chain of trust; Resolver DNSSEC validation
typical_risks: DNSSEC disabled for critical public hosted zones, KSK/KMS region or policy mistakes, missing or incorrect DS chain of trust, KSK ACTION_NEEDED status, and missing DNSSEC alarms.
service_intro: Route 53 cryptography configuration is primarily DNSSEC rather than TLS ciphers. The KSK works with an asymmetric KMS key to sign the DNSSEC chain of trust.
recommended_values: DNSSEC enabled for critical public zones

service: AWS Virtual Private Network - AWS Site-to-Site VPN
main_focus: IKEVersions; Phase1/Phase2 encryption algorithms; Phase1/Phase2 integrity algorithms; DH groups; PSK or private certificate authentication
typical_risks: IKEv1, weak DH groups, SHA1 integrity, long-term non-GCM compatibility encryption, unmanaged PSKs, and certificate authentication lifecycle gaps.
service_intro: Site-to-Site VPN cryptography configuration is mainly managed through IKE/IPsec tunnel options.
recommended_values: PSK in Secrets Manager or private certificate authentication

service: AWS Virtual Private Network - AWS Client VPN
main_focus: TLS 1.2+ managed VPN transport; server certificate in ACM; mutual authentication client certificate; CRL lifecycle
typical_risks: Expired or incomplete certificates, long-term RSA 1024-bit certificates, uncontrolled client certificate and private-key distribution, expired CRLs, and weak authentication governance.
service_intro: Client VPN uses TLS 1.2 or later for remote-access VPN connections. Users mainly control certificates, mutual authentication, and authorization rules.
recommended_values: mutual authentication where certificate-based client identity is required
valid CRL when revocation is used

crypto_category: N/A; relies on API TLS and downstream service encryption
main_focus: No direct TLS/cipher/key configuration; namespace discovery type; Route 53 DNS integration for DNS namespaces
typical_risks: Treating Cloud Map as an encryption or TLS termination boundary, leaving public DNS integrity to chance, placing sensitive data in metadata, or failing to enable TLS on downstream services.
service_intro: Cloud Map is a service discovery service and does not expose user-selectable cipher suites, TLS policies, or KMS key configuration.
recommended_values: Use HTTPS/TLS for AWS API access
Use private DNS namespace for internal discovery where appropriate
Apply Route 53 DNSSEC controls for public DNS integrity needs
Enable TLS on downstream service endpoints

main_focus: CreateKey.KeySpec; KeyUsage; Origin; MultiRegion; key rotation; key policy; ML-DSA post-quantum signing keys
typical_risks: KeySpec and KeyUsage mismatch, asymmetric or HMAC key misuse for AWS service encryption, legacy algorithms, missing rotation, unmanaged imported or custom-store material, and overbroad key policies.
service_intro: AWS KMS is the core AWS key management service. Cryptographic controls include KeySpec, KeyUsage, Origin, MultiRegion, rotation, and key policy.
recommended_values: ML_DSA_44/65/87 for PQ signing use cases
automatic rotation for eligible symmetric AWS_KMS keys
manual rotation plan for asymmetric/HMAC/imported/custom-store keys
api_endpoint_focus: CreateKey.KeySpec / KeyUsage / Origin / MultiRegion; EnableKeyRotation; PutKeyPolicy

main_focus: Cluster mode; HSM type; PKCS#11/JCE/CNG mechanisms; key attributes; crypto user ownership
typical_risks: DES3, SHA1, RSA-PKCS, or other legacy mechanisms used as a long-term baseline; overbroad key attributes; weak CU ownership; and non-FIPS clusters in regulated scenarios.
service_intro: CloudHSM is a dedicated HSM service controlled by the customer. Key risks live in client SDK mechanism choices, key attributes, CU permissions, and cluster mode.
recommended_values: FIPS mode where compliance requires it
api_endpoint_focus: CreateCluster.Mode / Client SDK mechanisms / key attributes

main_focus: RequestCertificate.KeyAlgorithm; CertificateTransparencyLoggingPreference; ExportCertificate.Passphrase; ImportCertificate lifecycle
typical_risks: RSA 1024 or obsolete certificate algorithms used as a baseline, imported certificates without managed renewal, incomplete chains, mismatched private keys, weak exported private-key handling, and CT logging exceptions.
service_intro: ACM cryptography configuration focuses on TLS certificate algorithms, certificate transparency logging, certificate import/export, and renewal governance.

main_focus: PutSigningProfile.platformId; signature algorithm family; signing profile validity; signing material for IoT workflows
typical_risks: SHA1-RSA platforms used as a new baseline, overbroad signing profile permissions, excessive signature validity, missing revocation or replacement workflows, and unmanaged IoT signing certificates.
service_intro: Signer cryptography configuration is mainly the signing algorithm and artifact format selected through the signing profile platformId.

crypto_category: Client-side encryption SDK; Keyring; Commitment policy
main_focus: commitment policy; AWS KMS keyrings; discovery keyring and discovery filter; encryption context
typical_risks: Non-committing algorithm suites for new encryption, KMS discovery keyrings without discovery filters, raw keyrings without strong key protection, and missing encryption context semantics.
service_intro: The AWS Encryption SDK is a general client-side encryption SDK. Important controls include commitment policy, keyrings, discovery filters, and encryption context.
recommended_values: AWS KMS keyring with explicit KMS key identifiers
discovery filter when discovery keyring is required
api_endpoint_focus: SDK client configuration: commitmentPolicy / keyring / discoveryFilter

crypto_category: Client-side encryption SDK; Searchable encryption; Keyring
main_focus: cryptographic actions; AWS KMS hierarchical keyring; beacons; searchable encryption planning
typical_risks: DO_NOTHING used for sensitive fields, irreversible action changes on existing populated fields, beacons with excessive leakage, and searchable encryption planned without the KMS hierarchical keyring.
service_intro: The AWS Database Encryption SDK provides field-level client-side database encryption. Key concerns are cryptographic actions, the KMS hierarchical keyring, and beacon leakage.
recommended_values: AWS KMS Hierarchical keyring for searchable encryption
api_endpoint_focus: table encryption config / cryptographic actions / beacon configuration / KMS hierarchical keyring

crypto_category: Client-side encryption SDK; Keyring; Wrapping key
main_focus: keyring; wrapping key; commitment policy; legacy wrapping algorithms; encryption context
typical_risks: Legacy wrapping algorithms for new encryption, raw AES/RSA wrapping keys without KMS or HSM-grade protection, permissive commitment policy, and unmanaged instruction files or encryption context.
service_intro: The Amazon S3 Encryption Client provides client-side envelope encryption for S3 objects, centered on data keys, wrapping keys, keyrings, commitment policy, and legacy wrapping algorithms.
recommended_values: AWS KMS wrapping key where possible
api_endpoint_focus: S3EncryptionClient builder keyring / wrapping key / commitmentPolicy

crypto_category: Payment cryptography; PIN; CVV; MAC; TR-31/TR-34; HSM
main_focus: CreateKey.KeyAttributes: KeyAlgorithm, KeyUsage, KeyModesOfUse; TR-31/TR-34/RSA/ECDH key import and export; PIN/CVV/MAC/data encryption operations
typical_risks: Overbroad KeyUsage or KeyModesOfUse, TDES_2KEY as a new baseline, NoRestrictions overuse, key exchange paths mismatched with compliance expectations, and weak exportable-key or KEK rotation governance.
service_intro: AWS Payment Cryptography is a specialized service for payment cryptography such as PIN, CVV, MAC, DUKPT, TR-31, and TR-34 workflows.
recommended_values: AES_128/AES_192/AES_256 where supported by the payment use case
TDES_3KEY when TDES is required for legacy payment interoperability
TR-34 or ECDH for KEK exchange where supported
TR-31 for working key exchange after KEK establishment

main_focus: Virtual node listener TLS; backend client TLS policy; mTLS certificate and trust validation
typical_risks: Listener TLS disabled or left in permissive migration mode; backend client TLS not enforced; weak mTLS trust chain, SAN validation, or certificate lifecycle governance; treating App Mesh TLS as a substitute for workload identity governance.
service_intro: AWS App Mesh cryptography configuration focuses on service-to-service TLS/mTLS inside the mesh, including listener TLS mode, backend client TLS policy, certificate sources, and trust validation.
recommended_values: mTLS with validated trust chain and SAN

main_focus: MACsec encryption mode; CKN/CAK key association; port/LAG MACsec capability and monitoring
typical_risks: MACsec disabled or no_encrypt; should_encrypt used as a long-term baseline; CKN/CAK material not managed through Secrets Manager and rotation controls; missing portEncryptionStatus monitoring; confusing link-layer MACsec with application end-to-end encryption.
service_intro: AWS Direct Connect cryptography configuration centers on optional MACsec, controlled through encryptionMode and MACsec key association for link-layer encryption.

main_focus: Managed AWS-to-partner MACsec boundary; partner network encryption assurances; application-layer encryption overlay
typical_risks: Treating AWS Interconnect managed MACsec as end-to-end encryption; not verifying partner network encryption or isolation commitments; sensitive workloads without TLS/IPsec overlay; missing audit for connection and routing changes.
service_intro: AWS Interconnect provides managed private connectivity through AWS partners. Unlike Direct Connect, it does not expose fine-grained customer MACsec key or cipher configuration, so analysis should focus on the managed encryption boundary, partner controls, and compensating end-to-end encryption.
recommended_values: Use managed Interconnect connection for private connectivity
api_endpoint_focus: CreateConnection / GetConnection / ListConnections; service data-in-transit protection boundary

main_focus: Gateway protocol HTTPS; responder trust store CA certificates; HTTP responder allowance; TLS 1.2+ RTB communications
typical_risks: Using HTTP or allowing HTTP responders as a long-term baseline; not enabling TLS for RTB application communications; missing or overly broad trust store CA configuration; placing credentials or sensitive information in URLs, tags, or free-form fields; mistaking service-managed encryption for application TLS and identity validation.
service_intro: AWS RTB Fabric is a specialized networking service for real-time bidding traffic. Cryptography-relevant configuration centers on TLS/HTTPS between RTB applications, responder gateway protocol and trust store, and whether links allow HTTP responders.

main_focus: Managed TLS 1.2+ from users to Verified Access; endpoint domain certificate ARN; endpoint protocol and backend encryption boundary
typical_risks: Expired or mismatched DomainCertificateArn; weak certificate lifecycle governance; HTTP backend paths for sensitive applications; assuming managed user-to-Verified-Access TLS covers backend hops and end-to-end encryption; trust provider or policy misalignment exposing an encrypted channel to the wrong principals.
service_intro: AWS Verified Access encrypts Internet traffic from end users to Verified Access endpoints using managed TLS 1.2 or later. Customer-controlled cryptography-relevant areas are endpoint domain certificate governance, application domain matching, endpoint protocol, and the backend encryption boundary.
recommended_values: HTTPS or TCP with backend encryption for sensitive applications
TLS/IPsec overlay where backend confidentiality requires it

main_focus: KMS encryption key; secret rotation; secret value versus metadata boundary; resource/KMS policy controls
typical_risks: Using the default aws/secretsmanager key where independent authorization or audit is required; rotation disabled or unverified; broad KMS key policy or secret resource policy; sensitive data in unencrypted metadata such as secret name, description, or tags; unclear KMS governance for replicated secrets.
service_intro: AWS Secrets Manager cryptography configuration centers on AWS KMS envelope encryption, encryption key selection, secret rotation, and access control. Risks focus on weak KMS governance, missing rotation, metadata leakage, and unclear multi-Region encryption boundaries.
recommended_values: Customer managed KMS key where independent control is required

main_focus: IAM access keys; server certificates; signing certificates; SAML provider certificates and encrypted assertions; password/MFA credential controls
typical_risks: Long-term access keys not rotated or unused keys left active; using IAM server certificates instead of ACM where ACM is supported; expired, incomplete, or mismatched certificates; SAML X.509 certificates and encrypted assertion private keys not monitored or rotated; weak password policy or missing MFA.
service_intro: IAM is not a TLS cipher-suite configuration surface, but it manages cryptography-relevant identity credentials such as access keys, server certificates, signing certificates, SAML certificates, MFA devices, and password policy.
recommended_values: ACM for server certificates where supported

crypto_category: SAML; X.509 Certificate; SCIM token; MFA
main_focus: SAML X.509 certificate rotation; SCIM access token rotation; MFA enforcement; external IdP trust boundary
typical_risks: SAML 2.0 X.509 certificates near expiry without rotation; expired or unmanaged SCIM access tokens; unclear MFA responsibility between external IdP and IAM Identity Center; assuming all SAML encrypted assertion scenarios are supported; application signing certificate rotation without overlap testing.
service_intro: IAM Identity Center cryptography-relevant configuration focuses on identity federation trust material rather than cipher-suite selection: X.509 certificates, SCIM bearer tokens, MFA, and external IdP trust boundaries.
api_endpoint_focus: IAM Identity Center SAML certificate management / SCIM access token rotation / MFA settings
